titleStmt.title: Centering Digitality. An interdisciplinary and discursive research network

keywords.term: digital research infrastructures development and analysis
meta-criticism (reflections on digital humanities and humanities computing)
I plan to attend the conference on-site in Lisbon

p.hi: The poster presents the concept and rationale of the newly installed Interdisciplinary Centre for Digitality and Digital Methods at Campus Mitte of Humboldt Universität zu Berlin (IC D2MCM).

The IC focuses both on digitality and digital methods as an epistemical construct and bridges diverse fields such as Digital Humanities (DH), social sciences, media and cultural studies, law, economics, and religious studies
. Our centre is a hub for interdisciplinary collaboration, connecting researchers, educators, and students
from six faculties and an institute within HU Berlin and beyond the university’s borders
. Our work emphasises digitality as a central research objective, fostering theoretical exploration and practical applications with a strong focus on AI and data literacy in DH and related fields

. The centre, even though we are still in an early implementation phase, fosters collaboration between otherwise not connected fields and colleagues (cf. this contribution; workshops on git or LLM and different outputs of working groups

) and is able to function as a broker within the HU Berlin for our requirements on organisational level. In addition, we aim to complement the existing landscape of Digital Humanities centres and to contribute to the vibrant discussions and research about the development of DH team, centres and labs within the community (cf. current discussions e.g. Pawlicka-Deger and Thomson 2024).

The methodological spectrum of Digital Humanities often addresses all aspects of the entire research data life cycle (cf. for an overview Cox and Tam 2018): design (e.g. Bode 2023, early Biber 1998 and Hunston 2008), annotation modeling and application (e.g. Ciula et al. 2023, Flanders and Jannidis 2019, Zeldes 2018), analysis (quantitative, qualitative, close or distant reading e.g. Primorac et al. 2023, Graham et al. 2022, Jänicke et al. 2015), and visualisation (e.g. Bubenhofer 2020, Drucker 2020) in conjunction with appropriate research software (e.g. for search and visualisation Krause and Zeldes 2016, for analysis and visualisation Sinclair and Rockwell 2016, for data wrangling OpenRefine
, publishing tools like TEI Publisher

). There is also an intensive epistemic discussion about, e.g., the scope, reliability or/and conclusions with respect to the results and interpretation of data-based empiric approaches (cf. e.g. Da 2019). However, the rich and extensive methodological focus needs to be completed by a meta-binding theoretical concept: digitality.
Our centre’s goal to contribute to digitality as an epistemological construct is reflected by the
Reading and Writing Lab

which investigates digitality as a crucially interdisciplinary concept at the core of Digital Humanities. We consider this to be an essential condition for the adequate development and application of digital methods and data. Among the constitutive components of digitality in the humanities are:
modeling and representation
(cf. McCarthy 2004, Barberousse and Ludwig 2009, Heßbrüggen-Walter 2016),
algorithms
and
formalization
as well as fundamental
mediality

(e.g. Hiltmann 2022). We contextualise and critically reflect these aspects within philosophical (cf. Krämer 2024), historical (cf. Nyhan and Flinn 2016), and cultural and media studies (cf. Stalder 2016; Chun 2018; Siegert 2023, Miyazaki 2023). These constitutive components influence (in-)directly how we design, create and analyse data on the one hand. On the other hand, they (in-)directly set requirements for the methods and tools applied to the data. Consequently, digitality also relates in different ways to software (modeling, development and application). Our work contributes to understanding the interplay of these components leading to a theoretical concept of digitality. In addition, we would like to investigate the historical, social, and material conditions and potentialities of digitality. We are interested in how digitality transforms methodologies, reshapes epistemic categories, and opens new theoretical perspectives. By addressing these questions and offering interdisciplinary infrastructures, we enrich the DH community and promote innovative research.

note.hi: Faculty of Language, Literature and Humanities, Faculty of Arts and Humanities, Faculty of Economics and Business Administration, Faculty of Law, Faculty of Theology, Faculty of Humanities and Social Sciences, Berlin Institute for Islamic Theology:
accessed 27/11/2024. Co working space for students:
Research Institutions in Digital Social and Cultural studies and Humanities: RIDSCH:
E.g. collaboration working group Git+ : Odebrecht, Carolin, Thomas Krause, Martin Klotz, Christine Mooshammer, und Melanie Althage. „Corpus Data Teaching Workflow Board“. Zenodo, 5. Februar 2025.
Working Group Reading and Writing Lab: Digitality

listBibl.head: Bibliography
listBibl.bibl: Barberousse, Anouk / Ludwig, Pascal (2009): "Models as Fictions" in: Suárez, Mauricio (ed.): Fictions in Science. Philosophical Essays in Modeling and Idealizations. London / New York: Routledge 56-73.
Biber, Douglas (1993): ”Representativeness in Corpus Design“. In: Literary and Linguistic Computing (8): 243–257.

Bode, Katherine (2023): „What’s the Matter with Computational Literary Studies?“ Critical Inquiry 49, Nr. 4: 507–29. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.1086/724943 .
Bubenhofer, Noah (2020). Visuelle Linguistik: Zur Genese, Funktion und Kategorisierung von Diagrammen in der Sprachwissenschaft. Impulse und Tendenzen 90. Berlin u.a.: De Gruyter.

Chun, Wendy Hui Kyong (2018): „Queerying Homophily. Muster der Netzwerkanalyse“ Zeitschrift für Medienwissenschaft, Jg. 10. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.25969/MEDIAREP/2400 .

Ciula, Arianna/ Eide, Øyvind / Marras, Cristina / Sahle, Patrick (2023): Modelling Between Digital and Humanities: Thinking in Practice. 1. Aufl. Cambridge, UK: Open Book Publishers. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.11647/OBP.0369

Cox, Andrew Martin / Winnie Wan Ting Tam (2018): „A Critical Analysis of Lifecycle Models of the Research Process and Research Data Management“. Aslib Journal of Information Management 70, Nr. 2: 142–57. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1108/AJIM-11-2017-0251.
Da, Nan Z. (2019): „The Computational Case against Computational Literary Studies“. Critical Inquiry 45, Nr. 3: 601–39. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1086/702594.
Drucker, Johanna (2020): Visualization and interpretation: humanistic approaches to display. Cambridge, Massachusetts: The MIT Press.

Flanders, Julia / Jannidis, Fotis (2019): „Data modeling in a digital humanities context. An introduction“. In The shape of data in the digital humanities: modeling texts and text-based resources, edited by Julia Flanders and Fotis Jannidis, 3–25. Digital research in the arts and humanities. London ; New York: Routledge, Taylor & Francis Group.

Graham, Shawn / Milligan, Ian / Weingart, Scott B. / Martin, Kim (2022): Exploring Big Historical Data: The Historian’s Macroscope. 2nd ed. WORLD SCIENTIFIC. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.1142/12435 .

Heßbrüggen-Walter, St. (2016): „Modellierung: eine Begriffsbestimmung“, DHd 2016 Modellierung - Vernetzung – Visualisierung: Die Digital Humanities als fächerübergreifendes Forschungsparadigma. 3. Tagung des Verbands "Digital Humanities im deutschsprachigen Raum" (DHd 2016), Leipzig. 2017. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.5281/ZENODO.4645271 .

Hiltmann, Torsten (2022): „Vom Medienwandel zum Methodenwandel: Die fortschreitende Digitalisierung und ihre Konsequenzen für die Geschichtswissenschaften in historischer Perspektive“. In Digital History, edited by Karoline Dominika Döring, Stefan Haas, Mareike König, and Jörg Wettlaufer, 13–44. De Gruyter. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.1515/9783110757101-002 .
Hunston, Susan (2008): „Collection strategies and design decisions“. In Corpus Linguistics, edited by Anke Lüdeling and Merja Kytö, 1:154–68. 1. Berlin: De Gruyter.

Jänicke, Stefan / Franzini, Greta / Faisal Cheema, Muhammad / Scheuermann, Gerik (2015): „On Close and Distant Reading in Digital Humanities: A Survey and Future Challenges“. Eurographics Conference on Visualization (EuroVis) - STARs. The Eurographics Association. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.2312/EUROVISSTAR.20151113 .

Krämer, Sybille (2024): „Philosophieren über Digitalität: 10 Thesen“. In Digitale Medien und die Produktion von Wissenschaft, edited by Thomas Kurtz, Dorothee M. Meister, and Uwe Sander, 47:103–19. Medienbildung und Gesellschaft. Wiesbaden: Springer Fachmedien Wiesbaden. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.1007/978-3-658-42542-5_7 .

Krause, Thomas / Zeldes, Amir (2016): „ANNIS3: A New Architecture for Generic Corpus Query and Visualization“. Digital Scholarship in the Humanities 31, Nr. 1: 118–39. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1093/llc/fqu057.

McCarty, Willard (2004): „Modeling: A Study in Words and Meanings“. In A Companion to Digital Humanities, edited by Susan Schreibman, Ray Siemens, and John Unsworth, 254–70. Malden, MA, USA: Blackwell Publishing Ltd. DOI: https://doi.org/10.1002/9780470999875.ch19.

Miyazaki, Shintaro (2023): „En-countering AI as algorhythmic practice“, in Handbook of Critical Studies of Artificial Intelligence, edited by Simon Lindgren (Edward Elgar Publishing), 682–90. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.4337/9781803928562.00069 .
Nyhan, Julianne / Flinn, Andrew (2016): Computation and the Humanities. Towards an Oral History of Digital Humanities. Cham: Springer.
Pawlicka-Deger, Urszula / Thomson, Christopher (2024): Chapter The Promise of Laboratories: An Introduction to Digital Humanities Laboratories in the 21st Century. New York: Taylor & Francis.

Primorac, Antonija / Arias, Rosario / Patras, Roxana / Eglāja-Kristsone, Eva / Van Dalen-Oskam, Karina / Herrmann, Berenike / Schöch, Christof / François, Pieter (2023): „Distant Reading Two Decades On: Reflections on the Digital Turn in the Study of Literature“. Digital Studies / Le champ numérique 13, Nr. 1. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.16995/dscn.8855 .
Siegert, Bernhard (2023): Kulturtechniken. Rastern, filtern, zählen und andere Artikulationen des Realen, Baden-Baden: Rombach Wissenschaft, 2023.
Sinclair, Stéfan / Geoffrey Rockwell (2016): Voyant Tools. Web. http://voyant-tools.org/. Voyant Tools. Web. < http://voyant-tools.org/> [2025-04-22].
Stalder, Felix (2016): Kultur der Digitalität. Berlin: Suhrkamp, 2016. <https://www.suhrkamp.de/buch/felix-stalder-kultur-der-digitalitaet-t-9783518126790> [2025-04-22].

Zeldes, Amir (2018): Multilayer Corpus Studies. 1st ed. Routledge. DOI:
https://doi.org/10.4324/9781315112473 .
